title: Computer Hardware Components Overview
obj: Students will be able to identify the major internal and external hardware components of a computer system and describe the function of each.
content: the processor — fetches, decodes, executes instructions; cores and clock speed
volatile working memory; holds running programs and open data
HDD vs SSD; where data lives when the computer is off
the main circuit board connecting all components; buses and slots
converts AC wall power to DC voltages the components need
USB, HDMI, PCIe slots, audio jacks; how components communicate externally
assess: Label-the-diagram: given a PC interior photo, identify 10 components by name
Component function quiz: match each component to a one-sentence description
Short answer: why does a computer need both RAM and storage?

title: Connecting and Configuring Peripheral Devices
obj: Students will be able to connect, configure, and troubleshoot common peripheral devices including input and output peripherals.
content: keyboard, mouse, touchpad, webcam, microphone, scanner — connection types
monitors, speakers, projectors — signal types (HDMI, DisplayPort, VGA)
USB-A/B/C, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi — plug-and-play vs. manual driver install
what a driver is, how to update or roll back, Device Manager basics
adaptive keyboards, switch access, screen readers — why they matter
extending vs. mirroring the desktop; resolution and refresh rate
assess: Lab: connect keyboard, mouse, and external monitor to a computer; verify all function
Troubleshoot scenario: printer appears in Device Manager with a yellow exclamation — walk through the fix
Short answer: what is a driver and why is it required?

title: Internal Computer Components (Motherboard, CPU, RAM, BIOS)
obj: Students will be able to describe the purpose and key specifications of the motherboard, CPU, RAM, and BIOS/UEFI, and explain how they interact during the boot process.
content: chipset, form factors (ATX/mATX/ITX), slots (PCIe, RAM), SATA/M.2 connectors, CMOS battery
cores, threads, clock speed, cache levels (L1/L2/L3), TDP, socket types (Intel vs AMD)
DDR4 vs DDR5, MHz, CAS latency, dual-channel; how capacity affects multitasking
firmware that initializes hardware at boot; POST sequence; boot order; Secure Boot
BIOS POST → bootloader → OS kernel handoff; failure points
heatsinks, thermal paste, case airflow; why CPU throttles when overheating
assess: Spec comparison: given two motherboard spec sheets, identify which supports DDR5 and PCIe 5.0
BIOS walkthrough: enter BIOS on a lab PC, change boot order, save and exit
Short answer: what does POST check, and what happens if it fails?

title: Internet Connection Service Types
obj: Students will be able to compare internet connection types by technology, speed, and use case, and recommend an appropriate connection for a given scenario.
content: Digital Subscriber Line; uses phone lines; asymmetric upload/download; typical speeds
coaxial cable shared with TV service; DOCSIS standard; faster than DSL; shared bandwidth
fastest consumer option; symmetric speeds; GPON/EPON; limited availability
4G LTE and 5G; hotspot devices; latency and data caps; good for mobility
Starlink and legacy geostationary; high latency; last-resort rural option
line-of-sight radio towers; used in rural/suburban gaps
assess: Scenario recommendation: given three household scenarios, recommend the best ISP technology and justify
Speed-test lab: run a speed test and record upload, download, and latency; compare to advertised speeds
Short answer: why does a fiber connection generally have lower latency than satellite?

title: Storage Technologies (HDD, SSD, NVMe, Cloud)
obj: Students will be able to compare HDD, SSD, and NVMe storage technologies by speed, durability, and use case, and describe how cloud storage fits into the storage hierarchy.
content: spinning platters, read/write heads, seek time, RPM (5400/7200); good for large cold storage
NAND flash cells (SLC/MLC/TLC/QLC), no moving parts, shock-resistant, faster than HDD
NVMe uses PCIe bus for much higher bandwidth; M.2 form factor; when the upgrade is worth it
RAID 0/1/5/10 — striping, mirroring, parity; RAID is not a backup
how services like OneDrive/Google Drive work; sync vs. backup; latency trade-off
workload-based selection: OS drive (NVMe), media library (HDD), backup (cloud)
assess: Comparison table: complete a 4-column table (HDD/SSD/NVMe/Cloud) comparing speed, cost per GB, durability, and best use
Lab: use CrystalDiskInfo or similar to read SMART data from a drive and interpret health
Short answer: why is RAID not a substitute for backup?

title: Computing Devices (Laptops, Servers, IoT)
obj: Students will be able to distinguish between different categories of computing devices — desktop, laptop, server, thin client, IoT — and identify the appropriate use case for each.
content: trade-offs: upgradeability, portability, battery, price per performance
high-end desktops for CAD/video/science; ECC RAM, multi-core CPUs, pro GPUs
rack-mounted and tower; redundant power, RAID, remote management (IPMI/iDRAC); always-on
minimal local processing; depends on server or cloud; used in VDI environments
smartphones, tablets — ARM processors, battery-first design, mobile OS
embedded systems, sensors, smart devices; limited compute; network-connected; security risk surface
assess: Scenario matching: given 5 workplace scenarios, select the correct device category and explain why
Short answer: why would a company deploy thin clients instead of full desktops?
Discussion: name two IoT devices in your home and describe the security risk each poses

title: Mobile Device Hardware and Connectivity
obj: Students will be able to identify mobile device hardware features, connection types, and accessories, and configure basic mobile device settings.
content: SoC (system-on-chip), LPDDR RAM, UFS storage, battery types, display tech (LCD vs OLED)
GSM vs CDMA legacy; LTE bands; 5G sub-6 vs mmWave; SIM vs eSIM
Wi-Fi 6/6E, Bluetooth 5, NFC (tap-to-pay, pairing), IR blaster
cases, screen protectors, docking stations, stylus, MagSafe/wireless charging
iCloud/Google backup; device-to-PC sync; MDM enrollment
USB-C, Lightning (legacy), MicroSD slots; what accessories require adapters
assess: Spec comparison: compare iPhone and Android flagship side-by-side on RAM, storage, connectivity
Configuration lab: on a practice device or simulator, enable hotspot, pair Bluetooth speaker, and back up to cloud
Short answer: what is a SIM card and what happens if you swap it between carriers?

title: Display Components and Attributes
obj: Students will be able to identify display technologies, describe key specifications, and select an appropriate display for a given use case.
content: TN, IPS, VA, OLED — viewing angles, response time, contrast, price trade-offs
1080p/1440p/4K; PPI; why resolution matters more on smaller screens
60 Hz vs 144 Hz vs 240 Hz; importance for gaming and smooth motion; VRR (G-Sync/FreeSync)
HDMI (1.4 vs 2.0 vs 2.1), DisplayPort, USB-C/Thunderbolt, VGA (legacy)
CCFL (legacy) vs LED backlight; local dimming zones; brightness (nits)
capacitive touch, stylus support, privacy screens, curved vs. flat
assess: Spec sheet exercise: given three monitor spec sheets, recommend one for a graphic designer and one for a call-center agent
Short answer: why would a video editor prefer IPS over TN?
Connection troubleshoot: a user plugs an HDMI cable into a DisplayPort port — what adapter is needed?

title: Cable Types and Connectors
obj: Students will be able to identify common cable types and connectors used in IT, select the correct cable for a given task, and explain the data-transfer limitations of each.
content: USB 2.0/3.0/3.1/3.2/4; connector types (A, B, mini, micro, C); transfer speeds; power delivery
HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI, VGA; max resolution and refresh rate per standard; active vs. passive adapters
3.5 mm TRS/TRRS, RCA, XLR, optical TOSLINK; balanced vs. unbalanced
Cat 5e/6/6a/8; RJ-45 connector; straight-through vs. crossover; bandwidth and distance limits
SATA data/power, SAS, PCIe/M.2 key types (M, B, B+M)
single-mode vs. multimode; LC/SC/ST connectors; used for long runs and high-speed backbone
assess: Cable identification lab: identify 10 physical cables/connectors by type and standard
Scenario: user wants to connect a 4K 144 Hz monitor — which cable should they use and why?
Short answer: what is the maximum length of a standard Cat 6 ethernet run before signal degrades?

title: RAM Types and Characteristics
obj: Students will be able to identify RAM form factors, describe DDR generation differences, and determine correct RAM upgrades for a given system.
content: full-size desktop DIMMs vs. small-outline laptop DIMMs; not interchangeable
DDR3/DDR4/DDR5 — voltage, speed (MT/s), bandwidth improvements; backward-incompatibility
error-correcting code RAM for servers and workstations; detects and corrects single-bit errors
installing RAM in matched pairs doubles memory bus width; performance benefit for integrated graphics
how much RAM is enough for common tasks; XMP/EXPO profiles for overclocking
assess: Upgrade scenario: user has 8 GB DDR4-2666 and wants to upgrade — what must they verify before buying?
Lab: use CPU-Z or Speccy to read RAM specs from a live system and fill in a spec sheet
Short answer: why can't you install a DDR4 stick in a DDR5 motherboard?

title: Installing Motherboards, CPUs, and Add-on Cards
obj: Students will be able to safely install a CPU, thermal solution, RAM, and PCIe card into a motherboard, following ESD precautions and verifying POST success.
content: anti-static wrist straps, mats, bags; touching the case; humidity; why ESD matters
locating the alignment notch/arrow; ZIF socket (Intel LGA vs AMD AM5); seating force; protecting pins
cleaning old paste, applying new paste (pea-sized or spread method); heatsink clip/screw-down
aligning the notch; pressing until clips click; dual-channel slot order
selecting the correct slot (x16, x8, x4, x1); securing with screw; driver install after boot
first POST; BIOS shows CPU, RAM; boot to OS; checking Device Manager for unknown devices
assess: Hands-on lab: install CPU, heatsink, and RAM into a practice board (or use a decommissioned machine)
Step-by-step checklist: given a scenario, list all safety steps before opening a PC case
Short answer: what does it mean if the PC powers on but immediately shuts off after a CPU install?

title: Power Supplies and Electrical Fundamentals
obj: Students will be able to identify PSU ratings, connectors, and efficiency standards, and determine whether a PSU is adequate for a given system build.
content: how a PSU converts 120/240V AC to 3.3V/5V/12V DC rails; multi-rail vs. single-rail
calculating system power draw; 80+ efficiency ratings (Bronze/Gold/Platinum); headroom rule of thumb
24-pin ATX, 4/8-pin CPU, 6/8-pin PCIe GPU, SATA power, Molex legacy
ATX, SFX, TFX; matching PSU form factor to case
cable management trade-offs; semi-modular as a middle ground
random shutdowns, burning smell, no POST, voltage instability; using a PSU tester
assess: Wattage calculation: given a parts list, use an online calculator to find minimum PSU wattage
Connector ID: identify 5 PSU connectors from photos
Short answer: a PC randomly shuts off under heavy load — why is the PSU the first suspect?

title: Printers, Multifunction Devices, and Maintenance
obj: Students will be able to describe printer technologies, perform routine maintenance tasks, and troubleshoot common printing problems.
content: laser (toner, drum, fuser), inkjet (cartridge types), thermal (receipt printers), impact (dot-matrix legacy)
processing → charging → exposing → developing → transferring → fusing → cleaning (7-step EP)
multifunction devices combining print/scan/copy/fax; network-shared vs. locally connected
print driver types (PCL vs PostScript); Windows print spooler; clearing stuck print jobs
toner/ink replacement, drum cleaning, paper feed rollers, calibration, firmware updates
USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi Direct, cloud print (Google Cloud Print successor, AirPrint, Mopria)
assess: Maintenance lab: replace toner cartridge, run a cleaning page, and run a calibration print
Troubleshoot scenario: user gets 'paper jam' but no paper is visible — walk through the diagnosis
Short answer: what is the fuser and what failure symptom does it cause when it fails?
